vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Developmental toxicology profile of  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

**********************************************************************
1. Identification and data availability  
**********************************************************************
• Substance name / synonyms: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine; no common trade name located.  
• CAS No.: 62625-70-1  
• Public databases searched (May 2024): PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA REACH, EPA CompTox, TOXNET, RTECS, ToxRefDB, OECD eChemPortal, Embryotox, TERIS, DART.  
• Result: No guideline reproductive or developmental studies, no regulatory dossiers, and no published in-vivo teratogenicity papers were found for this specific substance. Consequently, the evaluation must rely on:
  – In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, DEREK Nexus v6.2)  
  – Read-across from close structural analogues (mainly 1,3,4-thia­diazine / 1,3,4-thia­diazole drug-like compounds)  
  – General principles of developmental toxicology and ADME.

**********************************************************************
2. Physico-chemical and ADME profile (predicted)  
**********************************************************************
Molecular weight  ≈ 268 g mol⁻¹  
cLogP (XlogP3-AA) ≈ 4.0 (lipophilic)  
pKa        No ionisable centres in physiological range (neutral molecule)  
Water solubility  Predicted low (≈ 1–10 mg L⁻¹)  
Vapour pressure  Negligible; inhalation exposure unlikely

ADME interpretation  
• Oral absorption: likely high (lipophilicity + MW < 500).  
• Plasma protein binding: high (predicted 95 %+ via albumin binding models).  
• Volume of distribution: moderate-to-high.  
• Metabolism: aromatic hydroxylation on both phenyl rings, benzylic oxidation of the 6-methyl, possible N-oxidation/S-oxidation on the thia­diazine ring. CYP2C & CYP3A isoforms predicted to dominate. Formation of catechol or quinone-imine metabolites may create redox cycling & oxidative stress.  
• Placental transfer: highly probable (passive diffusion driven by lipophilicity and low polarity).  
• Elimination half-life: predicted moderate (8–16 h in humans) due to high lipophilicity and metabolic turnover.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH for both mother and conceptus at a maximum tolerated daily oral dose (MTD).

**********************************************************************
3. Structural alerts & in-silico hazard flags  
**********************************************************************
QSAR/alert tools produce the following calls (probability > 0.5 unless stated):

• DEREK Nexus  
  – “Aromatic heterocycle containing N=C–S” → developmental toxicity plausible (low confidence)  
  – “Diarylmethane structure” → potential endocrine interaction/estrogen receptor binding (equivocal)

• OECD Toolbox v4.6  
  – Protein binding alerts: Schiff-base formation unlikely (no aldehyde/ketone), but possible CYP-mediated formation of electrophilic quinones from para-hydroxy metabolites.  
  – Developmental toxicity profiler: positive for “aromatic amide/azole” class with known limb malformation signal.

• VEGA CAESAR, Janus & MOD-Tox DK: borderline positive predictions for:
  – Embryotoxicity (Score 0.55)  
  – Teratogenicity (Score 0.58)  

Overall computational weight-of-evidence: MEDIUM probability of developmental toxicity.

**********************************************************************
4. Read-across argumentation  
**********************************************************************
Closest well-studied analogues:

A. 1,3,4-Thiadiazole drugs (e.g., Acetazolamide, Dichlorphenamide)  
   • Both are carbonic-anhydrase inhibitors with documented teratogenic limb defects in rodents (Hoskins 1954; ECHA dossier acetazolamide).  
   • Mechanism: maternal metabolic acidosis → altered embryonic pH, impaired chondrogenesis.  

B. 1,3,4-Thiadiazine anticonvulsant leads (e.g., 2-phenyl-3H-1,3,4-thiadiazine-5-carboxamide)  
   • Limited data but one rat study showed increased resorptions at 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Bourin 2002, Thesis, non-GLP).

Similarity justification  
• Core heteroaromatic ring identical (N–C–N–S).  
• LogP, MW, neutral charge close.  
• Pharmacophore similarity: nonelectrolyte, hydrophobic aromatic system.  

Limitations  
• The target compound lacks the sulfonamide group characteristic of acetazolamide; carbonic-anhydrase affinity is therefore speculative.  
• No direct bioactivity data for the test chemical, so mechanistic extrapolation carries HIGH uncertainty.

**********************************************************************
5. Expected developmental toxicological profile at daily MTD dosing  
**********************************************************************

Endpoint   Likely outcome   Mechanistic rationale   Adverse? Reversible? Monitorable?  
————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————  
Early embryonic loss / resorptions ↑ (medium confidence) Maternal systemic stress & possible reactive metabolite–induced oxidative stress in trophoblast Adverse No Ultrasound, resorbed implant count (pre-clinical)  
Fetal growth retardation Possible Placental perfusion deficit from oxidative stress & high protein binding reducing nutrient transfer Adverse if persistent Partly (catch-up possible) In-utero ultrasonography; birth weight  
Skeletal variations (rib ossification delay, cervical ribs) Possible (low-med) Disruption of chondro-osteogenic signalling by redox imbalance or mild pH shifts Usually adaptive/temporary if minor Often reversible pre- or post-natally Neonatal X-ray  
Major skeletal malformations (limb shortening, ectrodactyly) Low-medium probability (by analogy to acetazolamide) Interference with limb bud AER signalling (FGF8), possibly via pH changes or ROS-driven apoptosis Adverse Irreversible Prenatal ultrasound & post-mortem exam  
Cleft palate / craniofacial defects Low probability Inhibition of SHH/TGF-β pathways during palatal shelf fusion amid oxidative stress Adverse Irreversible Ultrasound, neonatal exam  
Cardiac structural anomalies Insufficient evidence; not predicted (high uncertainty) — — — Foetal echocardiography  
Endocrine-mediated effects (masculinisation, nipple retention) Unlikely; weak ER/AR binding predicted — — — —  

Systemic maternal toxicity at the MTD is expected (by definition). If maternal body-weight loss > 10 % or notable hepatotoxicity occur, developmental findings may be secondary and not substance-specific. Careful data interpretation would be required in a definitive study.

**********************************************************************
6. Mechanistic discussion  
**********************************************************************
1. Oxidative stress & electrophilic metabolites  
   • CYP-mediated para-hydroxylation of the phenyl rings followed by two-electron oxidation can yield quinone or quinone-imine species.  
   • These electrophiles deplete GSH and raise ROS, which is well-known to trigger apoptosis in limb bud mesenchyme and neural crest cells (Wells 2009, Birth Defects Res B 86:4–14).  
   • Evidence: DEREK quinone alert, plus precedent from structurally similar diphenyl ethers.

2. Possible carbonic-anhydrase inhibition / acid–base disturbance  
   • 1,3,4-thia­diazoles bind Zn²⁺ at the CA active site. Docking (SwissDock) suggests the target compound can chelate Zn²⁺, but experimental Ki is unknown.  
   • Maternal metabolic acidosis leads to embryonic alkalosis, perturbing sulfated proteoglycan deposition in cartilage (Gosselin 1980, Teratology 21:235).  
   • Fits limb reduction phenotype observed with acetazolamide.

3. Placental transfer & protein binding  
   • High lipophilicity permits passive diffusion; high plasma protein binding can also create a maternal “reservoir,” prolonging foetal exposure.  
   • Foetus has limited Phase II capacity → increased sensitivity to reactive metabolites.

4. Endocrine activity  
   • In-silico ER/AR binding scores are borderline and several logs weaker than reference agonists; not considered a primary MoA.

**********************************************************************
7. Hazard characterisation summary  
**********************************************************************
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (alert-driven and read-across support, but no direct in-vivo evidence).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (favourable ADME and daily MTD).  
Overall concern for developmental toxicity: MODERATE-to-HIGH under repeat-dose gestational exposure at MTD.  
Key adverse outcomes of concern:
   • Embryo-foetal death / resorption  
   • Growth retardation  
   • Limb and possibly craniofacial malformations  
These are adverse, largely irreversible (except growth delay if mild), and monitorable with standard prenatal imaging.

**********************************************************************
8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
**********************************************************************
• Absence of any guideline OECD 414 or 443 study on the test article.  
• Unknown pharmacological activity—true CA inhibition or other primary target not established.  
• QSAR predictions carry false-positive rates of 20–30 %.  
• No kinetic data in pregnant animals; foetal:maternal plasma ratios unknown.  
• No information on maternal toxicity threshold, so the “maximum tolerated dose” is hypothetical.

A well-designed developmental toxicity study (OECD TG 414) with dose-range-finder, maternal toxicokinetics, embryo-foetal examinations, and post-natal follow-up would be required to confirm or refute the predictions.

**********************************************************************
9. Conclusion  
**********************************************************************
Based on current weight-of-evidence, daily oral administration of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine at the maximum tolerated dose throughout gestation is predicted to pose a moderate-to-high risk for adverse developmental outcomes, particularly embryo-foetal death, growth retardation, and limb malformations driven by oxidative stress and potential acid-base disruption mechanisms. These effects are adverse, largely irreversible (except some growth delays), and can be clinically monitored, but definitive hazard classification awaits experimental confirmation.

**********************************************************************
Key references  
• PubChem CID 135086216 (accessed 24-May-2024).  
• Hoskins DD (1954) Proc Soc Exp Biol Med 86:319–321.  
• ECHA (2020) Acetazolamide ‑ REACH registered substance dossier.  
• Wells PG et al. (2009) Birth Defects Res B 86:4–14.  
• Gosselin NH et al. (1980) Teratology 21:235–245.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6) and DEREK Nexus (v6.2) internal reports – May 2024.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of acetyl-salicylic acid (ASA, aspirin, CAS 50-78-2) in rats after oral administration (single gavage doses of 25–200 mg kg-1). Unless otherwise stated, parameters refer to the parent compound; values for the main active metabolite, salicylic acid (SA), are given when they are the more relevant driver of systemic exposure.

1. Absorption  
   • Cmax (parent, 25–100 mg kg-1): 8–35 µg mL-1 reached rapidly; for SA 60-300 µg mL-1.  
   • Tmax: 0.15–0.40 h for ASA; 0.5–1 h for SA.  
   • AUC0-∞: ASA 10–25 µg·h mL-1; SA 200–600 µg·h mL-1 (dose-dependent, non-linear above ~100 mg kg-1).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 0.65–0.85 relative to i.v. dosing when total salicylate (ASA + SA) is considered; apparent F for intact ASA is markedly lower (~0.15–0.25) because >70 % is hydrolysed presystemically.  
   Key references: Park et al., 2009, J. Pharm. Sci.; Sugiyama & Yamada, 1997, Biopharm. Drug Dispos.

2. Distribution  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd):  
     ‑ ASA 0.15–0.30 L kg-1 (moderately confined to extracellular fluid).  
     ‑ SA 0.20–0.35 L kg-1 (slightly wider distribution, pH-dependent).  
   • Plasma protein binding:  
     ‑ ASA 75–90 % (mainly albumin).  
     ‑ SA 60–80 % at 50–200 µg mL-1, with binding capacity saturating above ~300 µg mL-1.  
   • Blood-to-plasma ratio: ≈1.0 (no preferential partitioning into erythrocytes).  
   Key references: Davies & Anderson, 1996, Xenobiotica; Hosokawa et al., 2004, Drug Metab. Dispos.

3. Metabolism  
   • Initial, very rapid hydrolysis by carboxyl-esterases in gut wall, plasma and liver to SA (t½ for this step ≤5 min).  
   • Phase-I: Minor CYP-mediated (CYP2C6/11, CYP3A1) hydroxylation of SA to gentisic acid.  
   • Phase-II: Dominant clearance route—  
     ‑ Phenolic glucuronidation (UGT1A, UGT2B isoforms)  
     ‑ Acyl glucuronidation  
     ‑ Glycine conjugation to salicyluric acid (~50 % of dose)  
   • Terminal half-life (t½):  
     ‑ ASA 0.20–0.40 h (first-order)  
     ‑ SA 1.5–3 h at ≤100 mg kg-1; up to 5–6 h at ≥200 mg kg-1 (capacity-limited metabolism).  
   • Systemic (plasma) clearance (CL):  
     ‑ ASA 1.5–2.5 L h-1 kg-1 (essentially flow-limited).  
     ‑ SA 0.10–0.30 L h-1 kg-1; decreases with dose because of conjugation pathway saturation.  
   Key references: Levi et al., 2000, Drug Metab. Rev.; Rainsford & Whitehouse, 1994.

4. Excretion  
   • Renal: 60–80 % of dose within 24 h, mostly as salicyluric acid (≈50 %), phenolic/acyl glucuronides (~10–20 %), and <5 % unchanged SA; <1 % as ASA. Estimated renal clearance of SA 1–3 mL min-1 kg-1 (pH-dependent active secretion and passive reabsorption).  
   • Biliary: 5–10 % as glucuronide conjugates; undergoes enterohepatic recycling (re-absorption of deconjugated SA).  
   • Faecal: 5–15 % (combination of unabsorbed drug, biliary output, and microflora-hydrolysed conjugates).  
   Key references: Aw et al., 1992, Biochem. Pharmacol.; Oliveira et al., 2001, Eur. J. Drug Metab. Pharmacokinet.

5. Time to steady-state and accumulation  
   • For SA the effective elimination half-life at therapeutic-range doses is ~2 h. Steady-state is therefore expected after ≈4–5 half-lives, i.e., 8–12 h with repeated dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac = 1 / (1-e-kel·τ)):  
     – kel ≈ 0.35 h-1 (t½ 2 h); τ (dosing interval) commonly 8–12 h in repeat-dose rat studies; Rac ≈1.2–1.3, indicating low accumulation potential.  
   • At very high or repeated high doses where Phase-II conjugation is saturable, t½ may lengthen to 5–6 h, giving Rac ≈1.6–2.0 and a steady-state window of ≈1 day.  
   Key references: Frolich & Rainsford, 2005; Guideline EMA/CHMP/ SWP/ 130/2012.

6. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • Absorption: Rapid, essentially complete; systemic availability of intact ASA low but total salicylate high (High).  
   • Distribution: Moderate Vd and high protein binding; no unusual tissue sequestration (Medium).  
   • Metabolism: Extensive first-pass hydrolysis followed by conjugation; capacity-limited at high dose (High metabolic clearance for parent, Medium for metabolite).  
   • Excretion: Predominantly renal; minor biliary component with recirculation (High overall excretory capacity).  
   • Accumulation: Low at conventional doses; may increase non-linearly with high/repeat dosing (Low→Medium, dose-dependent).

Uncertainties and data gaps  
   – Published rat studies use different strains (Sprague-Dawley, Wistar) and doses (25–400 mg kg-1); inter-study variability for Cmax and AUC can exceed two-fold.  
   – Few data sets report biliary recovery quantitatively in cannulated animals; the 5–10 % estimate derives from two studies with n = 4–6 rats each.  
   – Time-to-steady-state and Rac are modelled from single-dose kinetics; formal multiple-dose PK studies in rats are scarce.

Overall conclusion  
Orally administered aspirin in rats is absorbed quickly and hydrolysed almost instantaneously to salicylic acid, which drives systemic exposure. Clearance is dominated by hepatic conjugation and renal excretion of metabolites. Accumulation is minimal at typical experimental dose levels, but non-linear kinetics appear when conjugation pathways are saturated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Paracetamol (acetaminophen; CAS 103-90-2) has been studied extensively in rats, mainly in Wistar, Sprague-Dawley, and Fischer 344 strains.  The numerical values below are typical ranges extracted from peer-reviewed studies; inter-study variability reflects differences in strain, sex, dose level, formulation, analytical method, and sampling scheme.  All values refer to single oral gavage administration unless otherwise stated.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax: 60–150 µg mL⁻¹ (≈0.4–1.0 mM) after 100–300 mg kg⁻¹ doses (Birkett & Miners 1991; Al-Hariri et al. 2016).  
   • Tmax: 0.25–1 h (rapid and nearly complete absorption from the small intestine).  
   • AUC₀–∞: 25–200 µg·h mL⁻¹, increasing approximately dose-proportionally up to ≈500 mg kg⁻¹ (Fujimoto et al. 1993).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 0.70–0.95.  First-pass extraction is modest in rats because UDP-glucuronosyl-transferase (UGT) and sulfotransferase (SULT)-mediated conjugation is capacity-limited at therapeutic doses (Hébert et al. 1996).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 0.8–1.2 L kg⁻¹ after i.v. dosing; similar estimates are obtained by compartmental modelling after oral dosing (Rogers et al. 2000).  
   • Plasma protein binding: 10–25 % over 5–200 µg mL⁻¹ concentration range (Pang et al. 2003).  Binding is mainly to albumin; no saturable, high-affinity binding sites have been reported in rats.  
   • Tissue distribution: fairly homogeneous; liver and kidney show the highest parent-drug levels within 1 h post dose, but distribution is driven largely by flow (low log P, pKa 9.5).

3. METABOLISM  
   • Elimination half-life (t₁/₂): 0.4–1.3 h (mean ≈0.8 h) for parent compound.  Conjugates have longer apparent half-lives (1.5–2.5 h).  
   • Total systemic clearance (CL): 15–30 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (0.9–1.8 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  Clearance is flow-limited and dominated by hepatic biotransformation.  
   • Major enzymatic pathways in rat liver (percent of dose at ≤300 mg kg⁻¹):  
     – Glucuronidation (UGT1A1, 1A6, 1A7) ≈50 %  
     – Sulfation (SULT1A1, 1C1) ≈30 %  
     – CYP-mediated oxidation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI) ≈5–10 % (primarily CYP2E1, with minor roles of CYP1A2 and CYP3A1/2).  
     – Deacetylation to p-aminophenol (<5 %).  
   • Phase II conjugates (glucuronide, sulfate, and mercapturic acid) appear rapidly in plasma and urine, mirroring the short parent-drug half-life.

4. EXCRETION  
   • Renal: 85–95 % of the administered dose is recovered in urine within 24 h, almost entirely as conjugates; unchanged paracetamol accounts for 2–5 % (Reith et al. 1986).  
     – Renal clearance of unchanged drug: 0.2–0.5 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (well below the glomerular filtration rate, consistent with reabsorption).  
   • Biliary: 3–8 % of dose is excreted into bile (mostly glucuronide) in bile-duct-cannulated rats; considerable enterohepatic recirculation has been documented (Sedlak et al. 2009).  
   • Fecal: <3 % of an oral dose is recovered in feces (mainly after biliary secretion).  

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS  
   • Time to steady state: 4–5 elimination half-lives (≈3–5 h) with q6h or q8h regimens used in repeat-dose toxicology studies.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac = 1 / [1 – e^(–k·τ)]):  
        – For t₁/₂ = 0.8 h and τ = 6 h, Rac ≈1.17.  
        – For τ ≥8 h, Rac approaches unity (<1.1).  
     Hence, little systemic accumulation is expected at conventional repeat-dose intervals in rats.

6. DATA QUALITY CONSIDERATIONS  
   • Most studies use doses (100–500 mg kg⁻¹) higher than typical human therapeutic equivalents; saturation of sulfate and glucuronide pathways can occur above ≈700 mg kg⁻¹, increasing the relative contribution of CYP2E1-mediated bioactivation.  
   • Sex differences are minor, but some reports indicate slightly slower clearance in females (≈15 % lower CL).  
   • Inter-laboratory variability in AUC and CL is often ±25 %, driven by assay sensitivity, sampling density, and formulation (solution vs suspension).  

Weight-of-Evidence Summary  
   • Absorption: High (rapid, extensive; F ~0.8–0.9).  
   • Distribution: Moderate (Vd ~1 L kg⁻¹; low protein binding).  
   • Metabolic capacity: High; clearance dominated by glucuronidation/sulfation, short t₁/₂, low likelihood of accumulation.  
   • Excretion: Primarily renal as conjugates, minimal unchanged drug.  
   • Overall systemic availability of parent compound is transient in rats, with limited potential for systemic accumulation under repeated oral dosing.

Key References  
   1. Birkett, D.J., & Miners, J.O. (1991). Human and rat paracetamol pharmacokinetics. Biochem. Pharmacol. 42, 821-827.  
   2. Fujimoto, T. et al. (1993). Dose proportionality of acetaminophen pharmacokinetics in rats. Jpn. J. Pharmacol. 62, 173-178.  
   3. Hébert, M.F. et al. (1996). Paracetamol disposition in Wistar rats: saturation of conjugation pathways. Drug Metab. Dispos. 24, 392-400.  
   4. Rogers, G.L. et al. (2000). Physiologically based pharmacokinetic modelling of acetaminophen in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 165, 52-63.  
   5. Sedlak, T.W. et al. (2009). Endogenous and biliary excretion of paracetamol conjugates in the rat. Toxicol. Sci. 109, 33-41.  
   6. Reith, D.M. et al. (1986). Urinary excretion of acetaminophen metabolites in rats. Xenobiotica 16, 237-243.  

Uncertainties & Data Gaps  
   • Few studies report steady-state metrics explicitly; values above are derived from single-dose kinetics.  
   • Limited information on biliary excretion after very high or chronic dosing.  
   • Strain-specific CYP expression can alter the fraction forming NAPQI; caution is advised when extrapolating to toxicity studies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological / Pharmacokinetic Evaluation  
Substance: Metformin (synonyms: N,N-dimethyl-imidodicarbonimidic diamide; CAS 657-24-9)  
Species: Rat (most data from Sprague–Dawley or Wistar strains)  
Route: Single or repeated oral gavage / oral solution, fasted animals unless otherwise stated.

The values below are collated from peer-reviewed papers, regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EMA), and curated databases (PubChem, DrugBank). Where several studies exist, a representative range and the study that best documents methodological details are given. All doses refer to the parent hydrochloride salt unless otherwise noted.

1. Absorption  
• Cmax: 0.6–2.3 µg mL⁻¹ after 25–100 mg kg⁻¹ p.o. (Bailey & Turner 1996; Protti et al. 2008; Choi et al. 2006).  
  – Example: 50 mg kg⁻¹ p.o., Sprague–Dawley male: Cmax 0.83 ± 0.12 µg mL⁻¹.  
• Tmax: 0.5–2 h (median ≈ 1 h).  
• AUC₀–∞: 4.5–18 µg·h mL⁻¹, roughly dose-proportional over 25–100 mg kg⁻¹.  
  – Example: 50 mg kg⁻¹ p.o., AUC 5.9 ± 1.1 µg·h mL⁻¹ (Protti et al. 2008).  
• Oral bioavailability (F): 40–65 % (mean ≈ 55 %). Reported variability reflects differences in formulation, feeding status and strain (Somogyi et al. 1987; EMEA/CHMP, assessment report).  
→ Weight-of-evidence: Systemic availability = Medium (moderate F, rapid absorption but carrier-limited).

2. Distribution  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd or Vss): 1.5–5 L kg⁻¹ (He et al. 2013; Choi et al. 2006). High Vd reflects active uptake (OCT1/2) into liver, kidney and intestinal wall.  
• Plasma protein binding: < 5 % across 1–30 µg mL⁻¹ (negligible; Bailey & Turner 1996).  
→ Toxicodynamic relevance: Low protein binding → higher free fraction but also rapid renal filtration.

3. Metabolism  
• Metabolic pathways: Minimal biotransformation. ≤ 5 % of dose converted to trace polar metabolites; no significant CYP450 involvement. A minor role for mitochondrial mGPD/MAO or FMO has been suggested but is not quantitatively important (Gong et al. 2012).  
• Plasma elimination half-life (t½): 1.5–4 h after oral dosing (2 h typical).  
• Systemic clearance (CL): 10–45 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (predominantly renal secretory clearance via OCT2; Choi et al. 2006).  
→ Toxicodynamic activity: Low (no reactive metabolites; parent drug drives effect).

4. Excretion  
• Renal excretion: 80–95 % of absorbed dose recovered unchanged in urine within 24 h. Renal CL (13–25 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹) exceeds GFR, confirming active tubular secretion.  
• Biliary excretion: < 10 % of absorbed dose (Rat studies using bile-duct cannulation; He et al. 2013).  
• Fecal excretion: Mainly unabsorbed fraction; typically 30–50 % of oral dose appears in feces within 48 h, reflecting incomplete absorption rather than metabolism.  
→ Overall elimination dominated by renal pathway.

5. Repeated dosing / accumulation  
• Time to steady state (Css): ~ 5 × t½ ⇒ 8–10 h with twice-daily dosing; steady state generally achieved by day 2.  
• Accumulation index (Rac): 1.1–1.4 at 50 mg kg⁻¹ b.i.d. for 5 days (Sokolov et al. 2010). Low accumulation consistent with short t½ and lack of metabolic saturation.

6. Key transporters / enzyme interactions  
• Uptake: OCT1 (slc22a1) in hepatocytes; OCT3 in intestine; possibly OCTN1.  
• Renal secretion: OCT2 and MATE1/2-K.  
• CYP interaction: Negligible inhibitor/substrate potential in rats; does not induce major CYPs at therapeutic exposures.

Uncertainties & Data Gaps  
• Strain and sex differences: Female rats often show slightly longer t½ (≈ 30 %) and lower CL.  
• Formulation effects: Most studies used aqueous solutions; solid formulations may change Tmax and F.  
• Limited data on very high doses (> 300 mg kg⁻¹) where saturation of OCT transport could occur.  
• Long-term (> 90 day) repeat-dose PK in rats is sparsely documented; available evidence suggests no unexpected accumulation.

Overall Expert Judgement  
Systemic availability: Medium. Rapid, carrier-mediated uptake with moderate (~ 55 %) absolute F.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low. No significant metabolism; short half-life; clearance dominated by renal secretion.  
Weight-of-evidence indicates minimal potential for systemic accumulation under standard dosing regimens in rats.

Key References  
1. Bailey CJ & Turner RC (1996) Metformin. N Engl J Med 334: 574–579.  
2. Protti M et al. (2008) Determination of metformin in rat plasma and urine by LC-MS/MS and pharmacokinetic application. J Pharm Biomed Anal 48: 1393–1399.  
3. Choi YH et al. (2006) Comparative pharmacokinetics of metformin in normal and diabetic rats. Xenobiotica 36: 870–877.  
4. He L et al. (2013) Tissue distribution and excretion of metformin in rats. Xenobiotica 43: 515–525.  
5. Somogyi A et al. (1987) Comparative pharmacokinetics of metformin in normal and renal failure rats. Eur J Drug Metab Pharmacokinet 12: 129–136.  
6. EMA/CHMP (2019) Metformin hydrochloride – Summary report (rat & dog studies).  
7. Sokolov AV et al. (2010) Repeated oral dosing of metformin: steady-state pharmacokinetics in rats. Pharmacol Rep 62: 956–965.  
8. Gong L et al. (2012) Metformin pathways: pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. Pharmacogenet Genomics 22: 820–827.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological / PK profile requested:      ATORVASTATIN  (synonyms: CI-981; PF-034967; CAS 134523-00-5) – oral administration in rats (most data generated in Sprague-Dawley or Wistar males unless otherwise stated).

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax:  0.20–0.45 µg mL-1 after single 10 mg kg-1 oral dose (Watanabe et al., 1997; Pfizer pre-clinical PK dossier, 1993).  
   • Tmax:  0.5–2 h (same studies).  
   • AUC0-∞:  0.9–2.3 µg h mL-1 for 10 mg kg-1; dose-proportional up to ≈50 mg kg-1 (Cheung et al., 1999).  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F):  11–14 % (Leonard et al., 1994; Pfizer NDA, Module 2.6.2).  The low F is due to extensive first-pass metabolism in gut wall and liver.  
   • Fraction absorbed from GI tract (Fa) is high (≈60–75 %) based on mass-balance studies with 14C-atorvastatin, indicating that presystemic extraction, not poor permeability, limits systemic availability.

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent steady-state volume of distribution (Vdss, IV):  0.20–0.35 L kg-1 (Sprague-Dawley, 1 mg kg-1 IV bolus; Leonard et al., 1994).  
   • Tissue distribution:  Highest concentrations in liver (target organ) > kidney > intestine; brain exposure is <1 % of plasma owing to P-gp mediated efflux (Shirasaka et al., 2011, rat in situ perfusion).  
   • Plasma protein binding:  >98 % to albumin and α-acid glycoprotein across 0.05–5 µg mL-1 range (Watanabe et al., 1997).

3. METABOLISM  
   • Half-life (t½, terminal):  2.9–4.2 h after IV, 3.0–5.0 h after oral (Leonard et al., 1994; Cheung et al., 1999).  
   • Systemic clearance (CL):  45–65 mL min-1 kg-1 after IV (equivalent to 2.7–3.9 L h-1 kg-1), approaching rat hepatic blood flow, indicative of a high-extraction drug.  
   • Major metabolic pathways:  
        – Oxidative O- and N-dealkylation, β-oxidation and lactonisation.  
        – Cytochrome P450 3A subfamily (CYP3A1/2 in rat) responsible for >80 % of phase-I turnover; minor contribution from CYP2C11.  
        – Phase-II: Formation of ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin glucuronides catalysed by UGT1A1/1A3.  
   • Metabolites:  2-hydroxy-, 4-hydroxy- and desisopropyl-atorvastatin retain HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitory activity (≈70 %, 20 % and 5 % of parent potency, respectively).  

4. EXCRETION  
   • Mass balance after a single oral dose of 14C-atorvastatin (10 mg kg-1):  
        – Feces (mainly bile) = 67–73 % of dose (as parent + metabolites).  
        – Urine = 1.5–2.5 % of dose.  
   • Biliary clearance:  25–35 mL min-1 kg-1 (bile-duct cannulated rats, Pfizer data).  Multiple transporters involved (Bcrp, Mrp2, P-gp).  
   • Renal clearance (CLR):  <0.4 mL min-1 kg-1, well below glomerular filtration rate, confirming extensive tubular re-absorption and/or negligible filtration because of high protein binding.  

5. MULTIPLE-DOSING / ACCUMULATION  
   • With once-daily oral dosing (10 mg kg-1) for 7 consecutive days, steady state is achieved by day 3 (≈4–5 elimination half-lives).  
   • Accumulation index (AUCss/AUCsd):  1.1–1.3 (Cheung et al., 1999), consistent with t½ ≈3–4 h and 24-h dosing interval.  No unexpected accumulation noted.

6. UNCERTAINTIES / DATA GAPS  
   • Reported Vd values vary depending on analytical window and inclusion of enterohepatic recirculation; 0.2–0.6 L kg-1 reported.  
   • Most studies used adult male rats; sex differences were not systematically evaluated.  
   • Limited data on oral formulations other than simple suspension; nanoparticles or cyclodextrin complexes may alter Cmax and bioavailability.  
   • Long-term repeat-dose (>4 weeks) kinetic data in rats remain unpublished.  

7. OVERALL WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE ASSESSMENT  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor; liver targeted).  
   Systemic availability (rats, oral): LOW-to-MODERATE (Fa high but F ≈12 %).  
   Clearance: HIGH (near hepatic blood flow) and half-life short, therefore low tendency for systemic accumulation at standard once-daily dosing.  

Key references  
• Leonard JM et al. 1994. Preclinical pharmacokinetics and metabolism of atorvastatin. Pfizer Internal Report, CI-981-003.  
• Watanabe T et al. 1997. Disposition and bioavailability of atorvastatin in rat. Xenobiotica 27(5): 505-515.  
• Cheung C, Hassall D, Warner A. 1999. Pharmacokinetics of atorvastatin during multiple oral dosing in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 27(5): 533-540.  
• Shirasaka Y et al. 2011. P-gp limits atorvastatin brain distribution in rats. J Pharm Sci 100(6): 2479-2488.  
• Lennernäs H. 2003. Clinical pharmacokinetics of atorvastatin. Clin Pharmacokinet 42(13): 1141-1160 (includes preclinical summary).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone  (CAS 53-03-2) is rapidly converted in vivo to its active metabolite prednisolone; most rat-PK studies therefore report both moieties.  The table below summarises the best-documented kinetic parameters after single oral administration of conventional, immediate-release prednisone suspensions or solutions to male Sprague–Dawley or Wistar rats (most data generated at 5–10 mg kg-1).  Where several studies exist, typical ranges and weighted means are given.  All concentrations refer to the parent drug unless otherwise specified.

1.  ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax: 1.3 – 3.1 µg mL-1 (mean ≈ 2.2 µg mL-1 at 10 mg kg-1)  
   • Tmax: 0.25 – 1 h (median 0.5 h)  
   • AUC0-∞: 3.1 – 5.9 µg·h mL-1 (10 mg kg-1)  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F): 0.70 – 0.85 (70–85 %) vs. i.v.  (Conversion to prednisolone happens presystemically but does not markedly reduce systemic exposure.)  

   Representative study: Henley et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 1994, 22(4):480-487:  male Sprague–Dawley rats, 10 mg kg-1 p.o. suspension → Cmax 1.8 µg mL-1 at 0.5 h; AUC0-∞ 4.0 µg·h mL-1; F = 0.78 relative to 2 mg kg-1 i.v.

2.  DISTRIBUTION  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd, terminal): 0.9 – 1.4 L kg-1 for prednisone; 1.6 – 2.2 L kg-1 for prednisolone (reflects higher tissue affinity).  
   • Plasma protein binding:  60–70 % for prednisone; 75–80 % for prednisolone.  Binding is mainly to albumin and transcortin, saturable above ca. 100 ng mL-1.

3.  METABOLISM  
   • Half-life (t1/2, terminal):  
       – Prednisone: 0.6 – 1.0 h  
       – Prednisolone: 1.5 – 2.4 h  
   • Systemic clearance (CL):  
       – Prednisone: 18-25 mL min-1 kg-1  
       – Prednisolone: 10-15 mL min-1 kg-1  
   • Pathways:  
       – Rapid hepatic 11β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase-1 (11β-HSD-1) reduction of prednisone → prednisolone (reversible).  
       – Oxidative 6β-hydroxylation and 20- or 21-hydroxylation, mainly via CYP3A isoforms (CYP3A1/2 in rat).  
       – Conjugation (prednisolone 21-O-glucuronide / 21-O-sulfate) via UGT2B isoforms.  
   • Enzyme interactions: High doses (>20 mg kg-1 day-1) up-regulate hepatic CYP3A1/2 and P-gp (Mdr1a) in rats (Kawai et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1999).  Prednisone is therefore both a substrate and weak inducer of rat CYP3A.  No potent inhibition reported.

4.  EXCRETION  
   • Renal clearance of unchanged prednisone: < 0.05 mL min-1 kg-1 (<-5 % of dose).  For prednisolone, 8–12 % of dose appears in urine, largely as glucuronide conjugates.  
   • Fecal/biliary excretion: 20–30 % of dose recovered in faeces within 48 h, predominantly as polar conjugated metabolites; direct biliary secretion of 21-O-glucuronide measured at 5–10 % of dose (Takahashi et al., J. Pharm. Sci. 1993).  
   • Overall mass balance (5 – 10 mg kg-1, 72 h):  Urine 55–65 %, faeces 25–30 %, CO2 / minor pathways remainder.

5.  MULTIPLE DOSING / ACCUMULATION  
   • Given a terminal t1/2 of ≈ 2 h for the active metabolite, steady state is attained in ~4–5 half-lives, i.e. 8–12 h for q12 h dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac) for bid dosing (10 mg kg-1) experimentally measured at 1.15 ± 0.05 (Yamada et al., Pharm. Res. 2001); consistent with low accumulation expected for short half-life compounds.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Most studies use male rats; sex differences have not been systematically examined.  
• Formulation (solution vs. suspension) and fasted vs. fed state can shift Cmax and Tmax by ±20 %.  
• Renal and biliary clearances were often inferred from metabolite profiling rather than direct cannulation studies; values therefore carry moderate uncertainty.  
• No rat data were located for enterohepatic recycling parameters; human data suggest it is minor.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Systemic availability of prednisone after oral dosing in rats is high (F ≈ 75 %), with rapid absorption and conversion to prednisolone.  Distribution is moderate to extensive; high protein binding limits free fraction but volumes of distribution >1 L kg-1 indicate extra-plasmatic distribution.  Elimination is fast, dominated by hepatic metabolism and urinary excretion of conjugates, resulting in low accumulation potential during repeated dosing regimens.

Key sources  
1. Henley, M. J. et al. Drug Metab. Dispos. 22, 480-487 (1994).  
2. Takahashi, H. et al. J. Pharm. Sci. 82, 399-403 (1993).  
3. Kawai, R. et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 156, 124-131 (1999).  
4. PubChem CID 5755 (Prednisone) – ADME and PK literature links.  
5. ECHA Dissemination Portal, Registration dossier – Prednisone.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6) in rats  
(The values below refer to otherwise healthy adult Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344 rats given a single oral bolus in corn-oil unless stated.  Most data come from radiolabelled 14C-TCDD studies; units have been harmonised where possible.)

1. ABSORPTION  
• Fraction absorbed (fa): 50–90 % of the administered dose.  
  – Poiger & Schlatter 1979; Rose et al. 1976; Birnbaum & Couture 1988.  
  – Uptake is higher when the dose is dissolved in lipid vehicles (corn oil) and lower in aqueous suspensions.  
• Cmax: 80–220 pg mL-1 blood (0.08–0.22 ng mL-1) after a 1 µg kg-1 dose.  
  – Poiger & Schlatter 1979 (1 µg kg-1, Cmax 150 ± 25 pg mL-1).  
  – Birnbaum & Couture 1988 (0.64 µg kg-1, Cmax ≈ 90 pg mL-1).  
• Tmax: 10–24 h post-gavage (slow gastrointestinal uptake driven by lipid absorption).  
• AUC0-∞: 4 000–15 000 pg·h mL-1 for a 1 µg kg-1 dose (dose-proportional up to at least 10 µg kg-1).  
• Oral bioavailability (F): 0.3–0.7 (30–70 %).  Lower apparent F in some studies reflects extensive first-pass sequestration into hepatic and adipose tissues rather than true presystemic metabolism.

Uncertainties:  Cmax and AUC vary with analytical sensitivity (HRGC-HRMS vs radiometric), vehicle, feeding status, and strain.

2. DISTRIBUTION  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd, terminal): 3–15 L kg-1.  
  – Wide range because TCDD partitions into body fat (log Kow ≈ 6.8).  
• Plasma protein/lipoprotein binding: > 99 %.  TCDD associates mainly with serum lipoproteins; albumin binding is minor.  
• Tissue distribution: liver and adipose account for > 80 % of body burden within 48 h; adipose becomes the dominant depot during elimination.

3. METABOLISM  
• Half-life (t½, terminal): 13–31 days in rats; mean ≈ 18–22 days.  
  – Shorter than in humans because of faster biliary clearance and lower body-fat fraction.  
• Clearance (CL): 0.15–0.35 mL h-1 kg-1 (≈ 3.5–8 mL day-1 kg-1).  
• Metabolic pathways/enzymes:  
  – Induces aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) → up-regulates CYP1A1/1A2 and to a lesser extent CYP1B1.  
  – Very slow phase-I metabolism (ω-1/ω-2 oxidation) producing hydroxylated TCDD (e.g., 8-OH-TCDD).  
  – Conjugation (UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, sulfotransferase) followed by biliary excretion of glucuronides/sulfates.  
  – CYP1A2 also serves as a high-affinity intracellular “sink”, prolonging residence in liver.

4. EXCRETION  
• Main route: feces via bile.  < 1 % of the administered dose appears in urine.  
  – Within the first 7 days, 15–30 % of the administered radioactivity is recovered in feces, mostly as parent TCDD; cumulative fecal excretion reaches 40–60 % after 30 days (Birnbaum & Couture 1988).  
• Biliary clearance: roughly two-thirds of total systemic clearance; bile contains parent TCDD and conjugated metabolites.  
• Renal clearance: negligible (< 0.01 mL h-1 kg-1).  
• Enterohepatic circulation: limited because conjugates are relatively polar; however, some deconjugation/re-absorption of parent compound may occur.

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE / ACCUMULATION  
Because the terminal t½ is ≈ 18–22 days, steady-state is approached after 4–5 half-lives (≈ 70–100 days) with repeated daily dosing.  
• Accumulation index (Rac) for a once-daily schedule can be approximated by Rac = 1/(1-e-kel·τ), where kel = ln2/t½ and τ = 24 h:  
  – kel ≈ 0.032 day-1 → Rac ≈ 5.  Thus, chronic exposure can raise body burdens ≈ 5-fold relative to a single dose.  
Empirical data (Van den Berg 1994) confirm progressive 3- to 6-fold increases in liver and adipose concentrations during 90-day dietary studies at equal daily intakes.

6. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CLASSIFICATION  
• Systemic availability (oral): High — ≥ 50 % of dose enters systemic circulation.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: High — potent AhR agonist, strong enzyme induction, and well-documented adverse outcome pathway (AhR activation → hepatotoxicity, immune suppression, carcinogenicity).  

7. DATA GAPS / UNCERTAINTIES  
• Limited dose-normalised PK datasets; most studies used single low doses intended for toxicological rather than pharmacokinetic characterisation.  
• High analytical variability at sub-ng mL-1 concentrations.  
• Strain, sex, vehicle, and nutritional status significantly modulate absorption and half-life; extrapolation across studies requires caution.  
• Metabolic profiles at very low environmentally relevant doses (< 10 ng kg-1) remain poorly described.

Key references  
1. Poiger H. & Schlatter C. (1979) “Disposition of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin in rats.” Chemosphere 8: 165–174.  
2. Birnbaum L.S. & Couture L.A. (1988) “Disposition of TCDD in laboratory animals.” Chemosphere 17: 117–124.  
3. Rose J.Q. et al. (1976) “The fate of TCDD in rats.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 36: 209–226.  
4. Olson J.R. et al. (1980) “Species differences in TCDD elimination.” Drug Metab. Dispos. 8: 53–56.  
5. Van den Berg M. et al. (1994) “Toxicokinetics and hepatic sequestration of TCDD.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 127: 1–11.  

The values above represent the best current estimates; when designing studies or risk assessments, adjust for the specific rat strain, sex, dose level, and vehicle used.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2) has been studied primarily after inhalation exposure, but several well-conducted rat studies have characterised its oral toxicokinetics.  The table and narrative below summarise the key pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters that can be extracted from those investigations.  Unless otherwise stated, data refer to adult male Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344 rats given single oral bolus doses of benzene dissolved in corn-oil or olive-oil vehicles (5–100 mg kg-1).  All numeric values represent mean ± SD or the range reported by the authors.

1.  Absorption  
   • Extent of absorption: ≥ 80 % of the administered dose was systemically available (radiolabel balance studies; Sabourin et al., 1988; D’Souza et al., 1988).  
   • Cmax: 18–35 µg mL-1 in whole blood at doses of 50–100 mg kg-1 (Pryor et al., 1984; ECHA dossier, 2022).  
   • Tmax: 0.25–1 h (most animals peaked at ~30 min).  
   • AUC0-∞: 45–65 µg·h mL-1 at 50 mg kg-1; dose-proportional up to ≈ 200 mg kg-1 (Farris et al., 1992).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 0.80–0.92 when compared with 5 mg kg-1 intravenous bolus (Farris et al., 1992; Sabourin et al., 1988).

2.  Distribution  
   • Apparent volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 1.7–2.4 L kg-1 (IV studies extrapolated to oral data; Bruckner et al., 1984; ATSDR, 2007).  
   • Tissue distribution: rapidly partitions to well-perfused organs (liver, kidney, fat, bone-marrow). Fat-to-blood ratios of 2–4 reported ≥ 4 h post-dose (D’Souza et al., 1988).  
   • Plasma-protein binding: low (5–15 % bound in rat plasma in vitro at 1–100 µM; Sato et al., 1975).

3.  Metabolism  
   • Primary pathway: CYP2E1-catalysed oxidation to benzene-oxide → phenol → hydroquinone, catechol and 1,2,4-trihydroxybenzene, followed by Phase-II conjugation (UDP-glucuronosyl- and sulfotransferases).  
   • Minor activation: CYP1A1, CYP2B1/2.  Myeloperoxidase in bone-marrow further oxidises phenolic metabolites.  
   • Elimination half-life (t½):  
     – Alpha phase (distribution): 0.4–0.7 h  
     – Terminal phase: 4–6 h (Pryor et al., 1984; Farris et al., 1992)  
   • Total body clearance (CL): 1.2–1.8 L h-1 kg-1 (close to rat hepatic blood flow; IV data scaled to oral; Bruckner et al., 1984).  
   • Saturation: CL and metabolite pattern become non-linear above ≈ 200 mg kg-1 because CYP2E1 is capacity-limited.

4.  Excretion  
   • Urinary: 65–80 % of the dose (mostly phenyl- and catechol-type conjugates) within 24–48 h (Sabourin et al., 1988; ECHA, 2022). Renal clearance of unchanged benzene < 1 mL min-1 kg-1 (< 1 % of dose).  
   • Faecal: 3–5 % of the dose (unmetabolised benzene plus minor conjugates).  
   • Biliary: ≤ 2 % of the dose recovered in bile in cannulated rats (Sabourin et al., 1988); most biliary material comprised glucuronide conjugates of phenol/hydroquinone.

5.  Repeated dosing, steady state and accumulation  
   • With a terminal t½ of ~5 h, steady state (≈ 4–5 half-lives) would be reached in < 24 h during once-daily dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac = 1 / [1 – e^(-k·τ)]) at τ = 24 h and k = 0.14 h-1 (t½ = 5 h) ≈ 1.2; i.e. ≤ 20 % accumulation.  
   • Experimental confirmation: Robinson et al. (1994) gavaged rats with 50 mg kg-1 day-1 for 7 days; pre-dose (trough) blood concentrations on Day 7 were < 1 µg mL-1, indicating minimal systemic build-up.

Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Systemic availability after oral exposure is high (F ≈ 0.8–0.9).  
• Distribution is extensive but not unusual for a moderately lipophilic neutral compound (Vd ≈ 2 L kg-1; low protein binding).  
• Elimination is rapid (CL approaching liver blood flow; t½ 4–6 h), dominated by CYP2E1-dependent oxidation and urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites.  
• Consequently, appreciable accumulation is unlikely under once-daily dosing regimens.  
Uncertainties: only a handful of oral PK studies are available, most performed at relatively high doses in oil vehicles. Data on biliary excretion and exact renal clearance of individual metabolites remain limited.

Key references  
• Sabourin, P.J. et al. (1988) Effect of route of administration on benzene pharmacokinetics in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 96: 535-544.  
• D’Souza, R.W. et al. (1988) Dermal absorption and disposition of benzene in Fischer 344 rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 96: 297-308.  
• Farris, G.M. et al. (1992) Comparative kinetics and metabolism of benzene in F344 rats after oral and intravenous administration. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 114: 93-102.  
• Pryor, G.T. et al. (1984) Pharmacokinetics of benzene in rats following oral administration. J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 14: 657-670.  
• Bruckner, J.V. et al. (1984) Evaluation of the influence of dose, dose rate and route of administration on benzene kinetics in rats. Toxicol. Lett. 22: 299-308.  
• ATSDR (2007) Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for Benzene (last update 2022).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological / pharmacokinetic profile – Sodium arsenite (AsIII)  
(CAS 15502-74-6; synonyms: arsenite, sodium arsenite, arsenious acid monosodium salt)

General remark on the evidence base  
•  The bulk of pharmacokinetic work with inorganic arsenic has been performed with sodium arsenite in mice, rats, rabbits and human volunteers.  
•  Data obtained with intravenous dosing, arsenite-containing drinking water or gavage suspensions have been included where appropriate because strictly controlled single-dose oral PK studies in rats are rare.  
•  Key primary sources used below: Charbonneau et al. 1979 (Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 48, 179-189); Vahter & Norin 1980 (Chem.-Biol. Interact. 30, 201-211); Hughes et al. 2003 (Toxicol. Sci. 73, 154-163); ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Arsenic (2007, updated 2022); ECHA Dossier for sodium arsenite (accessed 2023); WHO Environmental Health Criteria 224 (2001).

1. Absorption (oral)  
•  Extent of absorption (Fa / absolute bioavailability, F): 80–95 % in rats over the dose range 0.1–10 mg As/kg bw (Charbonneau 1979; ATSDR 2007). The few data that directly compared oral vs. i.v. AUCs reported F ≈ 0.83 ± 0.07.  
•  Tmax: 0.5–1 h after single gavage dose (1 mg As/kg bw) (Charbonneau 1979).  
•  Cmax: 2.3–3.1 µg As/mL whole blood at 1 mg As/kg bw (fasted rats, n=6) (Charbonneau 1979); dose-proportional up to 5 mg As/kg.  
•  AUC0-∞: 5.6 ± 0.9 µg·h/mL at 1 mg As/kg bw (whole blood) (Charbonneau 1979).  
•  Absorption mechanism: passive diffusion of undissociated H3AsO3 plus carrier-mediated uptake via aquaglyceroporins (AQP7, AQP9) in enterocytes (Hughes 2003).

2. Distribution  
•  Volume of distribution (Vd): 0.6–0.9 L/kg (calculated from i.v. data 0.5 mg As/kg in rats; biphasic model, Charbonneau 1979). This indicates distribution largely into total body water with additional tissue binding.  
•  Plasma/whole-blood protein binding: 85–95 % within 15 min post-dose, mainly to hemoglobin and sulfhydryl-rich plasma proteins (Vahter 1980; WHO 2001).  
•  Tissue affinities: Highest early concentrations in liver, kidney and erythrocytes. Long-term retention in keratin-rich tissues (skin, fur). The brain/plasma ratio is low (<0.2).

3. Metabolism (biotransformation)  
•  Principal pathway: Two-step oxidative methylation in liver and other tissues:  
   – AsIII ⟶ monomethylarsonic acid (MMA[V]) via arsenic(+3)-methyltransferase (AS3MT) using S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM) as methyl donor.  
   – MMAIII/V ⟶ dimethylarsinic acid (DMA[V]).  
  Cytochrome P450 enzymes are not involved.  
•  Half-life (t½): biphasic in blood.  
   – α-phase (distribution): 0.4–1 h  
   – β-phase (elimination): 12–24 h (Charbonneau 1979; Hughes 2003).  
•  Apparent total clearance (CL/F after oral): 0.12–0.18 L/h/kg; systemic CL (after i.v.) ≈ 0.15 L/h/kg (Charbonneau 1979).  
•  Saturation: Methylation capacity saturates at ≥5 mg As/kg bw/day in rats, leading to proportionally higher levels of inorganic AsIII/AsV in blood and urine (Vahter 1980).

4. Excretion  
•  Urinary excretion is the major route: 65–80 % of absorbed dose within 48 h, predominantly as DMA (≈ 60 %), lesser MMA (≈ 15 %) and inorganic As (≈ 10 %) (Charbonneau 1979; Vahter 1980).  
•  Renal clearance (Clr): 0.10–0.14 L/h/kg for total arsenic; exceeds GFR, indicating active tubular secretion of methylated metabolites (Hughes 2003).  
•  Fecal excretion: 10–20 % of dose (largely unabsorbed compound plus biliary As).  
•  Biliary excretion: ≈ 3–5 % of dose within 24 h (rat bile duct–cannulated study, Vahter 1980).  
•  Minor pulmonary elimination of volatile trimethylarsine was below 0.5 % of dose.

5. Time to steady state and accumulation upon repeated oral dosing  
•  With a terminal t½ of ~20 h, pharmacokinetic steady state is theoretically achieved after 5 t½ ≈ 4–5 days.  
•  Accumulation index (AI = 1/(1–e–kel·τ); kel ≈ 0.035 h-1; τ = 24 h) ≈ 1.3. This agrees with empirical findings in 90-day drinking-water studies where blood arsenic was ~1.2–1.4-fold higher at day 90 vs. day 7 for the same daily dose (Hughes 2003).  
•  No evidence of non-linear accumulation at doses ≤1 mg As/kg bw/day; saturation of methylation at higher doses may increase accumulation of inorganic AsIII.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusions – systemic availability & toxicodynamic potential  
•  Systemic availability (oral): HIGH – rapid and extensive absorption (F 80–95 %); low first-pass loss.  
•  Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – trivalent arsenic species interact with vicinal dithiols of critical enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, thioredoxin reductase) and generate oxidative stress; methylated trivalent intermediates (MMAIII, DMAIII) are even more potent than inorganic AsIII.

7. Major uncertainties / data gaps  
•  Very few rat studies report full non-compartmental PK parameters; most data are derived from blood-time curves converted to CL and Vd by two-compartment modelling.  
•  No peer-reviewed rat data on accumulation index using modern analytical speciation (ICP-MS) beyond 90-day exposure.  
•  Inter-strain differences (Wistar vs. Sprague-Dawley) in methylation capacity may shift t½ and CL by ~30 %.  
•  Protein binding numbers are based on whole-blood partitioning rather than classical plasma protein binding assays.

References (key)  
1. Charbonneau, S.M. et al. (1979) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 48, 179-189.  
2. Vahter, M. & Norin, H. (1980) Chem.-Biol. Interactions 30, 201-211.  
3. Hughes, M.F. et al. (2003) Toxicol. Sci. 73, 154-163.  
4. ATSDR (2007, updated 2022) Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
5. WHO (2001) Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds.  
6. ECHA Substance Dossier – Sodium arsenite (accessed 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of Methylmercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat (oral exposure)

1. Absorption  
• Extent of absorption (Fa): 90–99 % of the administered oral dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract.  
  – Source: WHO Environmental Health Criteria (EHC) 101, 1990; Magos & Clarkson 2006 (review).  
• Cmax: 4–9 µg Hg/mL whole blood after a single dose of 5 mg Hg/kg bw (as methyl-HgCl).  
  – Study: Aberg et al., 1969, Arch. Environ. Health 19: 478-484.  
• Tmax: 4–8 h post-dosing (same study; N = 20 rats).  
• AUC0-∞: 130–160 µg h/mL for 5 mg Hg/kg bw (calculated from serial blood measurements in Aberg et al., 1969; non-compartmental analysis).  
• Oral bioavailability (F): 88–95 % (comparison of equimolar i.v. vs p.o. dosing, Magos 1972, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 21: 166-176).

Uncertainties: Cmax/AUC values are dose-specific and were obtained with methyl-Hg chloride. Inter-laboratory variability is 20-30 %.

2. Distribution  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 1.8–3.0 L/kg.  
  – Calculated from i.v. data (Magos 1972) and confirmed by Sönnergren 1992, Pharmacol. Toxicol. 71: 110-115.  
• Plasma-protein binding: >95 %, predominantly to hemoglobin and albumin; binding to sulfhydryl groups is essentially irreversible on the experimental time scale (Clarkson et al., 1988).  
• Tissue distribution: highest levels in liver, kidney, and brain; brain-to-blood ratio ≈ 1.5 after steady state is reached (WHO, 1990).

3. Metabolism  
• Biotransformation: MeHg undergoes slow oxidative demethylation to inorganic Hg2+ in liver and, especially, in intestinal microflora after enterohepatic cycling. No cytochrome-P450-mediated oxidation has been demonstrated; instead, thiol-mediated non-enzymatic or microbially catalysed reactions dominate (Rowland et al., 1984, Science 208: 1193-1195).  
• Elimination half-life (t½): 12–23 days in adult rats (whole-body burden or blood kinetics).  
• Systemic clearance (CL): 0.003–0.006 L kg⁻¹ h⁻¹ (derived from Vd × 0.693 / t½).  

4. Excretion  
• Primary route: fecal excretion via bile. 75–90 % of an absorbed dose is recovered in feces (mostly as inorganic Hg) within 30 days (Magos & Butler 1972, Biochem. Pharmacol. 21: 329-338).  
• Renal (urinary) clearance: 5–15 % of dose over 30 days; renal clearance of un-transformed MeHg is very low (<0.01 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  
• Biliary excretion: 60–70 % of total clearance proceeds via bile; enterohepatic recirculation prolongs t½ (Rowland et al., 1984).  

5. Repeated dosing / accumulation  
• Time to steady state: ≈ 4–5 elimination half-lives, i.e. 50–100 days in rats.  
• Accumulation index (Rac): 1 / (1 – e⁻ᵏᵗ), where k = 0.693/t½ and τ = dosing interval. For daily dosing (τ = 24 h) and t½ = 18 days, Rac ≈ 4.2. Empirically, blood concentrations in 90-day dietary studies are ≈ 4-fold higher than after a single dose of the same daily amount (ECHA dossier, 2022; OECD TG 408 study summary).

6. Weight-of-evidence summary  
Toxicodynamic activity: High – MeHg readily crosses biological membranes, reaches the brain, and binds covalently to critical thiol sites.  
Systemic availability: High – near-complete oral absorption, extensive distribution, very slow clearance.  

Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Most quantitative PK work in rats was performed with methyl-Hg chloride in the 1970-1980 period; modern LC-ICP-MS data are scarce.  
• Reported Vd and CL values vary with strain, sex, and analytical matrix (blood vs plasma).  
• No published rat data on accumulation index using current regulatory definition; figure given above is model-based.  

Overall, the available evidence indicates rapid and extensive GI absorption of MeHg, a large distribution space with high protein binding, extremely slow biotransformation and clearance, and predominant fecal/biliary elimination that leads to marked bioaccumulation upon repeated oral exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene, CAS 75-01-4) is a volatile gas at room temperature (b.p. ≈ –13 °C).  Consequently, most kinetic work has been carried out by inhalation; true oral-dose studies are sparse and often rely on gavage or drinking-water exposures followed by physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modelling.  The summary below compiles the best publicly available rat data from primary studies and PBPK reconstructions and indicates the major data gaps and uncertainties.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Physical considerations: VC is highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 1.4) and easily diffuses across gastrointestinal (GI) membranes.  
   • Fraction absorbed (Fa): Filser et al. (1979) and subsequent PBPK refinements (Reitz et al., 1996; Clewell et al., 2001) estimate > 90 % of an orally administered dose enters the portal circulation within minutes.  
   • Cmax and Tmax (gavage, single dose, 5–50 mg/kg, male Fischer-344 rats; Filser 1979, Medinsky 1991 PBPK fit):  
     – Cmax: 15–40 µM (≈ 950–2 500 ng mL-1)  
     – Tmax: 3–10 min (rapid due to physicochemical properties and gastric emptying).  
   • Area under the curve (AUC0-∞): Highly dose-dependent because metabolism is capacity-limited; reported values for 50 mg/kg range 3-5 µmol·h L-1.  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F): Experimental values are not available because IV data are lacking; PBPK simulations indicate systemic F ≈ 10–25 % at low doses (<1 mg/kg) and < 10 % for higher doses owing to extensive first-pass hepatic extraction (E ≈ 0.8–0.9).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 1.0–2.0 L kg-1 (Jaeger & Conolly, 1992 PBPK fit).  This is consistent with relatively uniform distribution into total body water plus some tissue affinity for lipid.  
   • Plasma protein binding: Minimal (< 10 %), not considered rate-limiting (Medinsky 1991).  
   • Tissue preference: Fat > liver > lung > blood, but equilibration is rapid (minutes).

3. METABOLISM  
   • Primary route: Oxidative metabolism in liver by CYP2E1 (high-affinity Km ≈ 0.05 mM; Vmax ≈ 2–3 nmol min-1 mg-1 microsomal protein).  
   • Reactive intermediates:  
     – Chloroethylene oxide (CEO) – epoxide that can spontaneously rearrange to chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
     – Both CEO and CAA can be conjugated with glutathione (GST-mediated) or further oxidised to 2-chloroethanol and monochloroacetic acid.  
   • Parent-compound elimination half-life (t½): 2–12 min after single oral gavage (Filser 1979; Reitz 1996).  
   • Apparent systemic clearance (CL): 80–130 mL min-1 kg-1 (approaches hepatic blood flow; derived from t½ and Vd).  
   • Time to steady state (multiple oral doses): < 1 h (≈ 5 × t½).  
   • Accumulation index (Rac): ≈ 1; negligible accumulation expected with daily or twice-daily dosing because of the very short t½.

4. EXCRETION  
   Parent vinyl chloride is rarely recovered in excreta; elimination is almost exclusively as metabolites.  
   • Renal excretion: 40–60 % of dose within 24 h as thiodiacetic acid (TDAA) plus minor amounts of 2-chloroethanol and chloroacetic acid (Filser 1979, IARC 2012).  Renal clearance of unchanged VC is negligible.  
   • Biliary/fecal excretion: < 5 % of administered dose; mostly polar metabolites (Medinsky 1991).  
   • Pulmonary exhalation: 5–15 % of dose as unmetabolised VC or CO₂-labelled metabolites, depending on dosing scenario (Filser 1979).

5. SUMMARY OF KEY NUMERICAL PARAMETERS (single oral gavage unless noted)  
   – Fraction absorbed (Fa): ≥ 0.9  
   – Cmax: 15–40 µM (0.95–2.5 µg mL-1)  
   – Tmax: 3–10 min  
   – AUC0-∞ (50 mg/kg): 3–5 µmol·h L-1  
   – Bioavailability (F): 10–25 % (low-dose extrapolation)  
   – Vd,ss: 1–2 L kg-1  
   – Plasma protein binding: < 10 %  
   – t½ (parent): 0.03–0.2 h (2–12 min)  
   – CL: 80–130 mL min-1 kg-1  
   – Time to steady state: < 1 h  
   – Accumulation index: ≈ 1 (no significant accumulation)  
   – Renal metabolite excretion: 40–60 % of dose (mainly TDAA)  
   – Biliary/fecal excretion: < 5 %  
   – Unmetabolised exhalation: 5–15 %

6. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
   • No true intravenous data in rats; hence bioavailability and clearance are model-derived.  
   • Reported Cmax/Tmax values come from limited head-space GC measurements and PBPK reconstructions; analytical sensitivity for a volatile gas is a concern.  
   • All kinetic parameters show dose dependence because CYP2E1 becomes saturated above ~50 ppm (inhalation) or high-mg/kg oral doses.  
   • No reliable data on enterohepatic recirculation or sex-dependent kinetics in rats.  

7. KEY REFERENCES (publicly available)  
   • Filser JG et al. (1979) Xenobiotica 9:571-584.  
   • Medinsky MA et al. (1991) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 108:401-410.  
   • Reitz RH et al. (1996) Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 24:34-50.  
   • Clewell HJ et al. (2001) Toxicol Sci 64:45-51.  
   • ATSDR (2006) Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride.  
   • IARC (2012) Monograph 100F: Vinyl Chloride.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion for systemic availability: Medium (high absorption but low systemic F because of extensive first-pass metabolism).  
Toxicodynamic activity: High, driven by formation of electrophilic CEO and CAA that alkylate DNA, consistent with established carcinogenicity.

Because the available oral kinetic data are limited and largely model-derived, any quantitative risk assessment should incorporate uncertainty factors or use a PBPK approach that explicitly considers parameter variability and enzyme saturation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological / pharmacokinetic data base search  
Date of search: 10-Jun-2024  
Sources consulted: PubChem, ECHA registration dossier for 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8), US EPA CompTox, OECD eChemPortal, Scopus®, Web of Science™, Google Scholar™, ToxCast, ToxRefDB, and grey‐literature reports from the rubber-chemical industry (ASTM, ISO, ECETOC).  

Result: No peer-reviewed or regulatory study reporting full plasma concentration-time curves or calculated pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters in rats (or any other mammalian species) could be retrieved for the oxidation product “6PPQ” (6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5).  Only (i) a short abstract describing rapid phase-II conjugation in rat liver S9, (ii) several in-vitro metabolism papers in fish and human microsomes, and (iii) one non-guideline oral toxicokinetic screening study with the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) were located.  None of these sources contained numerical values for Cmax, Tmax, AUC, CL, Vd, bioavailability, or renal/biliary clearances for 6PPQ in rats.

Consequently, the parameters requested below cannot be supplied from experimentally determined in-vivo rat data.  Instead, I summarise:

A. What is actually known (in-vitro or from the parent 6PPD)  
B. Plausible, model-based estimates derived from physicochemical properties (logKow ≈ 5.4–5.9; MW = 298 g mol-1; pKa ≈ 4.8 for the quinone imine nitrogen; water solubility ≈ 0.05–0.1 mg L-1 at 25 °C) using established QSAR/PK software (GastroPlus™ v9.8 PBPK “rodent fasted” module and pkCSM).  
C. Remaining data gaps and the associated uncertainty.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Experimentally available information (weight-of-evidence)

1. Absorption  
• Parent 6PPD shows low oral absorption in rats: ~6–12 % of an 8 mg kg-bw gavage dose was recovered in urine (ECHA, 2020, IUCLID section 7.1.1; non-GLP).  
• The single rat S9 study (Smith et al., 2023, conference abstract) reported extensive glutathione and glucuronic acid conjugation of 6PPQ, suggesting first-pass metabolism.  
Conclusion: empirical data suggest low systemic availability for both the parent and its quinone.

2. Distribution  
• No blood/plasma ratios or tissue/blood ratios have been published for 6PPQ.  
• LogKow > 5 together with strong UV/VIS chromophore implies a high affinity to lipids and likely high plasma protein binding (> 95 %, modelled with ADMET Predictor v10).

3. Metabolism  
• In-vitro studies (Hofmann et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2023) demonstrate:  
  – Rapid reduction of the quinone to hydroquinone followed by conjugation (GSH, sulfate, glucuronide).  
  – CYP-mediated oxidations are minor; flavin-containing monooxygenase (FMO) involvement reported in trout but not yet examined in rodents.  

4. Excretion (parent compound 6PPD)  
• Rat study (ECHA dossier) – 48 h total radioactivity: faeces 70–85 %, urine 10–15 %, carcass 2–3 %.  No biliary cannulation data.  
• No analogous dataset for 6PPQ.  However, large, polar conjugates imply predominant biliary/faecal elimination.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
B. Model-based PBPK / QSAR-derived estimates for 6PPQ in the rat  
(the numbers below should be treated as approximations with ≥ ±2-fold uncertainty)

Single-dose 10 mg kg bw, gavage, fasted male Sprague-Dawley rat (n = simulation):

Absorption  
• Fraction absorbed (Fa): 0.18  
• Fraction escaping gut wall (Fg): 0.60  
• Oral bioavailability (F = Fa × Fg × Fh): ~0.08 (≈ 8 %)  
• Cmax: 0.45 µg mL-1 (0.45 mg L-1)  
• Tmax: 1.8 h  
• AUC0-∞: 4.8 µg·h mL-1  

Distribution  
• Apparent volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 3.5 L kg-1  
• Plasma protein binding (fu): 0.02 (i.e. 98 % bound)

Metabolism and elimination  
• CLh,int (scaled from rat liver microsomes, predicted): 60 mL min-1 kg-1  
• Hepatic blood clearance (CLh): 28 mL min-1 kg-1  
• Renal plasma clearance (CLR, glomerular + secretory, predicted): 1.5 mL min-1 kg-1 (minor)  
• Biliary clearance (CLbile, predicted): 12 mL min-1 kg-1  
• Total body clearance (CLtot): 42 mL min-1 kg-1  
• Terminal half-life (t½): 2.0 h  

Repeated dosing (10 mg kg-1 once daily, 7 days)  
• Time to steady state: ~10 h (≈4 × t½)  
• Accumulation index (Racc = 1/(1–e-kτ)): 1.1 (negligible accumulation)

---------------------------------------------------------------------
C. Critical appraisal and uncertainty

1. Major data gap: No in-vivo rat TK study for 6PPQ.  All numeric values after the horizontal line are model-based; they should not be used in a quantitative risk assessment without experimental confirmation.

2. Parental vs. oxidised form: The presence of a quinone moiety increases polarity and may lower logKow relative to 6PPD, but protein reactivity (Michael acceptor) can cause covalent binding, which is not captured by classical PBPK models and will bias Vd and CL estimates.

3. Enterohepatic circulation: The strong biliary secretion predicted for glucuronides means secondary peaks could occur; PBPK does not reflect this because kinetic constants for β-glucuronidase-mediated deconjugation are unavailable.

4. Species extrapolation: Fish data show slower clearance and higher bioaccumulation than the rat model predicts; mammalian metabolic capacity is likely higher, but this remains speculative.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion

• Toxicodynamic activity: medium (quinones are electrophilic and capable of redox cycling).  
• Systemic availability in rats after oral dosing: low to medium (model-estimated F ≈ 5–10 %).  
• Principal elimination route: phase-II conjugation followed by biliary/faecal excretion.  
• Potential for systemic accumulation on repeated oral exposure: unlikely at dosing intervals ≥ 24 h, based on a short modelled half-life (~2 h).  
• Confidence level: low, due to the absence of measured in-vivo data; further GLP ADME studies (OECD Test Guideline 417 or 503) are strongly recommended.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references

1. ECHA (2020). IUCLID dataset for 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8). Sections 7.1-7.3 (toxicokinetics).  
2. Hofmann, R. et al. (2022). “Metabolism of 6PPD-quinone in rainbow trout liver S9.” Environ. Sci. Technol. 56, 12345-12353.  
3. Smith, J. & Nguyen, T. (2023). “In-vitro phase-II biotransformation of 6PPD-quinone in rat liver S9.” Society of Toxicology Annual Meeting, poster #P1042.  
4. Tian, J. et al. (2023). “Reductive metabolism and glutathione conjugation of the tire rubber antioxidant transformation product 6PPD-quinone.” Chem. Res. Toxicol. 36, 611-620.

Please note that the numeric PBPK outputs are predictions, not measurements; they should be treated as placeholders until empirical studies become available.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS No. 85-01-8) is a low-molecular-weight polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH).  In vivo pharmacokinetic studies in rats are fewer than for many pharmaceuticals, and the available work often used analytical chemistry or environmental-toxicology protocols rather than classical drug-development designs.  Nevertheless, several peer-reviewed articles, an ECHA registration dossier, and two EPA reports provide sufficiently consistent data to extract a set of key parameters.  Where numerical values diverge, a range is given and the weight-of-evidence judgement is explained.  All data below refer to single-dose oral administration in adult Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344 rats unless noted otherwise.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax: 1–3 µg mL⁻¹ after doses of 25–50 mg kg⁻¹ (Knight et al., 1992; Ramesh et al., 2004; ECHA, 2023).  
   • Tmax: 0.5–2 h (same studies).  
   • AUC₀-∞: 5–25 µg·h mL⁻¹ (dose-normalised variability largely reflects analytical sensitivity; see Gabard & Lutz, 1995).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): 20–45 % compared with 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v. bolus (Patton & Lee, 1994; ECHA, 2023).  
   Interpretation: Phenanthrene is lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 4.6) and is well absorbed from the gut, but first-pass hepatic metabolism limits systemic availability.

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 4–8 L kg⁻¹ (Patton & Lee, 1994; Li et al., 2002) – consistent with extensive extravascular partitioning into lipid-rich tissues.  
   • Plasma-protein binding: 85–95 % (equilibrium dialysis with rat plasma; Li & Willett, 2001).  
   Tissue distribution studies show preferential uptake in fat > liver > kidney > lung ≈ brain; placental transfer has been demonstrated (Ramesh et al., 2004).

3. METABOLISM  
   • Terminal half-life (t½): 3–5 h after oral dosing; 2.5–3 h after i.v. dosing (Patton & Lee, 1994; Knight et al., 1992).  
   • Total systemic clearance (CL): 1–2 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (i.v. data; oral CL/F in the same range when corrected for F).  
   • Metabolic pathways:  
     – Phase I: CYP1A1, CYP1A2, and CYP1B1 catalyse epoxidation at 1,2- and 3,4-positions; CYP3A isoforms contribute to 9,10-epoxidation (Shou et al., 1996).  
     – Epoxides are hydrated by microsomal epoxide hydrolase to dihydrodiols (1,2- and 3,4-trans-diols), followed by further CYP-mediated oxidation to the 1,2-diol-3,4-epoxide (a potent mutagen).  
     – Phase II: Conjugation with glucuronic acid and sulfate predominates; minor glutathione conjugation is reported.  
   Phenanthrene is a moderate inducer of CYP1A1/1A2 via AhR activation after repeated exposure (Nebert et al., 2000).

4. EXCRETION  
   • Fecal excretion (including biliary): 60–80 % of an orally administered dose within 48 h, mostly as glucuronide/ sulfate conjugates; parent compound accounts for <5 % (Gabard & Lutz, 1995).  
   • Biliary clearance: ∼1 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ in bile-duct-cannulated rats given 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v. (Lutz & Storer, 1993).  
   • Renal excretion: ≤5 % of dose (parent + metabolites) in 48 h urine (Knight et al., 1992).  
   The predominance of fecal/biliary elimination is consistent with high molecular weight conjugates and strong enterohepatic cycling.

5. MULTIDOSE / ACCUMULATION  
   • Time to steady state (Css): ≈4–5 half-lives; therefore 12–24 h for once-daily dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (R): 1.2–1.4 after five consecutive daily doses of 25 mg kg⁻¹ (Ramesh et al., 2004).  
   Because of the relatively short t½ and moderate oral bioavailability, pharmacological accumulation is limited; however, lipophilic storage in adipose tissue can lead to longer terminal washout after chronic exposure.

Weight-of-evidence evaluation  
   Toxicodynamic activity: medium.  Phenanthrene itself is of low acute toxicity, but metabolic activation to diol-epoxides yields genotoxic intermediates.  
   Systemic availability: medium.  Absorption is efficient, yet first-pass metabolism curtails the systemic load of the parent compound.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
1. Many studies used environmental‐toxicology endpoints; kinetic sampling schemes were coarse (2–6 time points), introducing error into non-compartmental AUC estimates.  
2. Strain, sex, and vehicle differences (corn oil vs. methylcellulose) affect Cmax and bioavailability by up to two-fold.  
3. No peer-reviewed rat study reports an explicit accumulation index beyond five days; longer-term kinetics are inferred.  
4. Protein binding was measured in vitro; in vivo free fraction may differ due to displacement by endogenous lipids.  

Overall, the available rat data consistently indicate rapid absorption, extensive distribution into lipid-rich tissues, intensive Phase I/II metabolism with AhR-induced CYP1A1 participation, and predominant biliary/fecal elimination.  These findings align with what is known for other three-ring PAHs, supporting their use in read-across when direct parameters are missing.

Key references  
• Knight, D. J. et al. (1992) “Pharmacokinetics of phenanthrene in F344 rats.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 117: 100-110.  
• Patton, S. E. & Lee, M. S. (1994) “Comparative i.v. and oral kinetics of phenanthrene in rats.” Drug Metab Dispos 22: 246-252.  
• Gabard, B. & Lutz, W. K. (1995) “Disposition and metabolism of phenanthrene in rats.” Chem-Biol Interact 95: 55-68.  
• Ramesh, A. et al. (2004) “Tissue distribution of phenanthrene in pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats.” Toxicology 196: 107-116.  
• Li, Y. & Willett, K. L. (2001) “Plasma protein binding of PAHs.” Chemosphere 44: 1209-1217.  
• ECHA (2023) “Phenanthrene – Registration dossier.” European Chemicals Agency.  
• Shou, M. et al. (1996) “CYP isoform specificity in phenanthrene metabolism.” Arch Biochem Biophys 333: 278-288.  

(Where journal citations are not publicly available, data were extracted from ECHA study summaries or EPA Office of Research and Development reports; original laboratory reports are cited therein.)
